Metric: Software name? (string)
URL? (URL)
Affiliation (institution(s)) (string or N/A)
Response: National Institute of Advanced Industrial Science and Technology (AIST), Japan; primary developer: Noriaki Ando (AIST Intelligent Systems Research Institute); OpenRTM-aist Project team at AIST

Metric: Software purpose (string)
Response: Robotic Technology Middleware (RTM) implementation for the OMG-standardized RT (Robot Technology) Component standard; provides a component-based framework for creating, managing and connecting RT-Components (robot software modules); enables distributed robot systems by abstracting hardware and software interfaces into standardized components; includes component framework, execution context management, CORBA-based inter-component communication, and development tools (RTCBuilder, RTSystemEditor); widely used in Japanese robotics industry and research

Metric: Number of developers (all developers that have contributed at least one commit to the project) (use repo commit logs) (number)
How is the project funded? (unfunded, unclear, funded∗) where ∗ requires a string to say the source of funding
Initial release date? (date)
Last commit date? (date)
Status? (alive is defined as presence of commits in the last 18 months) (alive, dead, unclear)
License? (GNU GPL, BSD, MIT, terms of use, trial, none, unclear, other∗) ∗ given via a string
Platforms? (set of Windows, Linux, OS X, Android, other∗) ∗ given via string

Software Category? The concept category includes software that does not have an officially released version. Public software has a released version in the public domain. Private software has a released version available to authorized users only. (concept, public, private)
Development model? (open source, freeware, commercial, unclear)
Publications about the software? Refers to publications that have used or mentioned the software. (number or unknown)
Source code URL? (set of url, n/a, unclear)
Programming language(s)? (set of FORTRAN, Matlab, C, C++, Java, R, Ruby, Python, Cython, BASIC, Pascal, IDL, unclear, other∗) ∗ given via string
Is there evidence that performance was considered? Performance refers to either speed, storage, or throughput. (yes∗, no)
Additional comments? (can cover any metrics you feel are missing, or any other thoughts you have)
Response: OpenRTM-aist is the de facto standard robotics middleware in Japan, implementing the OMG RT-Component standard (OMG formal/08-01-07). A separate Python implementation (OpenRTM-aist-Python) provides full Python RT-Component framework. There is also OpenRTM-aist-Java. The OMG standardization means OpenRTM components are interoperable with other RT-middleware implementations. Used in Japanese humanoid robots (HRP series), industrial robots, and research platforms. Eclipse-based development tools (RTCBuilder, RTSystemEditor) support GUI-based component development.

Response: pkg-setup.sh auto-install script is very convenient (1 command). The CORBA dependency (omniORB) is the primary complexity factor compared to simpler middleware. Japanese-language documentation is comprehensive; English documentation is also provided but less complete. pip install openrtm-aist-python (3 commands) for Python users is simpler.

Response: CORBA-based middleware has proven reliability for distributed systems. AIST institutional maintenance ensures high-quality releases. CORBA naming service dependency requires specific startup sequence.

Metric: Does the software handle unexpected/unanticipated input (like data of the wrong type, empty input, missing files or links) reasonably? (a reasonable response can include an appropriate error message.) (yes, no∗ )
For any plain text input files, if all new lines are replaced with new lines and carriage returns, will the software handle this gracefully? (yes, no∗, n/a)
Overall impression? (1 .. 10)
Additional comments? (can cover any metrics you feel are missing, or any other thoughts you have)
Response: CORBA-based IPC is inherently robust for distributed systems. Component lifecycle management (initiate, activate, deactivate, finalize) handles state transitions safely. Production robot use validates robustness.

Response: Comprehensive Japanese documentation available at openrtm.org; English documentation adequate but less complete than Japanese. Eclipse-based RTCBuilder GUI tool aids component development. CORBA concepts can be steep learning curve for new users outside Japan.

Response: AIST government laboratory institutional maintenance provides reliable long-term support. November 2024 release confirms active maintenance. Dual LGPL/EPL+commercial license model provides sustainable funding path. METI government support continues.

Response: OMG standard compliance ensures interoperability with other RT-middleware implementations. CORBA IDL interface definitions provide language-neutral component specifications. LGPL license enables commercial use with some restrictions; commercial license removes restrictions. Python implementation (OpenRTM-aist-Python) increases accessibility.
